name: Microsoft Excel
description: The working spreadsheet environment behind many property budgets, variance analyses, reconciliations, and capital-planning models.
whyUseIt: Even in system-heavy environments, Excel remains the place where assumptions are tested, budgets are modeled, recoveries are pressure-tested, and management packs are assembled quickly. Strong spreadsheet discipline is still one of the fastest ways to become useful in property management.
aiCapabilities: AI can summarize tables, suggest formulas, explain drivers of variance, and accelerate first-draft analysis for budgets and reports.
workflowStage: Budgeting, analysis, reconciliation, and financial storytelling
propertyManagerWorkflow: Managers use Excel to draft budgets, test assumptions, model scenario impacts, clean export data, and prepare sharp financial commentary.
managerExpects: Structured files, reliable formulas, clear assumptions, and workbooks that someone else can follow without guessing.
aiWorkflowImpact: AI can speed up first-pass analysis, but the manager still needs to validate formulas, logic, and commercial conclusions.
demoDescription: Take a prior-month budget report, isolate the three biggest cost variances, and write one line of commentary for each.

vocabulary.definition: The difference between actual performance and plan or prior period.
A test showing how one changed assumption affects the final financial outcome.
An adjustment method used in some lease structures to normalize recoverable costs.

beginnerMistakes: Hard-coding numbers into formulas with no assumption trail
Using complicated tabs when one clean model would do
Sending spreadsheets without clear version control or notes
